summary: I worked for the Atlantic Coast Line Railroad for many years, starting on the section gang and later as a watchman at a busy crossing. My duties included stopping traffic when trains passed, and I took pride in keeping the crossing safe with no accidents since I took over. I remember working on the extra gang cutting rails and cooking for workers, and now I live in a section house along the main line near Lake Wire. The railroad has been central to my life and livelihood.

I recall the Atlantic Coast Line Railroad Limited train passing through on its way to Tampa, Florida. The train would kick up a lot of dust as it sped by, and I had to warn drivers to step back for their safety. Tampa was a known destination on the route I worked along, marking an important point on the railroad line.

I live with my family in a section house at Peel Lake, Lake Drive, right beside the main line of the railroad. It's a modest place, but it’s home for us and close to where I work. The house is company-provided and painted gray with white trimmings, like the other section houses along Lake Wire.

Two of my stepchildren, Mildred and Jennie Lee, live at home in Washington, D.C. They have stayed close to family while I have moved around Florida for work. Washington represents a place where part of my family has settled away from the railroad life.

My stepchild Lee Early is married and lives in Trilby, Florida. That’s one of the places my family has spread out to while I have continued working on the railroad in various parts of Florida. Trilby is part of my extended family’s story.

I was born in Jefferson County, Florida, on December 5th, 1866, and my parents were farmers there. I ran away from home as a boy because of my stepmother’s treatment but later returned as a man. I married my first wife, Rosetta Turner, in Monticello, Jefferson County, in 1901, and I also worked on farms there with my father. Jefferson County is where my roots and early life are deeply planted.

Julia Stern was my father's second wife after my mother passed away. She was my stepmother, and I had a difficult time with her, which led me to run away from home when I was about twelve or thirteen. My relationship with Julia Stern influenced my early life decisions and shaped my youth.

Rosetta Turner was my first wife; we married in Monticello, Jefferson County, in 1901. We eventually separated in later years. During that time, I settled in Alachua County and worked on the railroad section gang. Rosetta was part of my early adult life before I remarried.

Monticello, Florida, is where I married my first wife, Rosetta Turner, in 1901. It’s a place tied to my early adult life and family beginnings. I also have roots in Jefferson County, where Monticello is located, and it was part of my farming and family background.

Jeanette Billman is my second wife; we married in 1926 in Lakeland, Florida. She is a pleasant woman and a good housekeeper, and we live together in the section house near the railroad. Though we have no children together, she is my partner in life and home.

Lakeland, Florida, is where I have lived and worked for many years now. After moving here in 1925, I continued my work on the railroad section gang and currently serve as a watchman at a busy crossing. Lakeland is home for me and my family, and I have a good reputation in the community here.

During my first marriage, I lived in Alachua County and worked on the railroad section gang there. It was a time when wood burner engines were used, and I earned $1.50 a day. This period was part of my early railroad career before moving on to other jobs in Florida.

I worked at the Kebblevale Phosphate Mine in Polk County for about three years. The job paid around seven dollars a week, and we lived in company quarters built for the workers. This was a different kind of labor before I returned to railroad work and farming.

I spent some time in Moorehaven, Glades County, working on the Atlantic Coast Line extra gang cutting down railroad rails. I also cooked for the workers during that period. Moorehaven was part of my varied work experience before settling into section gang work in Lake Wales and Lakeland.

Glades County is where Moorehaven is located, and I worked there on the Atlantic Coast Line extra gang. It was a tough job cutting rails and cooking for the crew. This county represents a chapter in my working life before I moved on to other railroad jobs.

I worked on the section gang headquartered in Lake Wales, Florida, from January to November 1925. This job was part of my railroad career before moving to Lakeland. Lake Wales was an important place in my work history on the Atlantic Coast Line Railroad.

Lake Wire is the area where my section house is located, right next to the Atlantic Coast Line Railroad. The houses there are painted gray with white trim, and it’s where I live with my family. The proximity to the railroad makes it convenient for my work as a watchman.

My brother Jim works as a watchman at the other crossing at Florida Avenue. We started working in service together, and he is now forty-seven years old. Florida Avenue is part of the railroad crossings where my family is involved in safety work.

I have been a member of the Primitive Baptist Church since 1915, and our pastor is Rev. B. Bartley. The church is located in Wacahoota, Florida, and I have served as an usher, board member, and trustee. My faith and church life are very important to me, and I believe in the old-fashioned religion we practice.

Rev. B. Bartley is the pastor of the Primitive Baptist Church that I belong to. He leads our congregation in Wacahoota, Florida, where I have been an active member for many years. His leadership has been a steady influence in my religious life.

Wacahoota, Florida, is where the Primitive Baptist Church I attend is located. I have been a member there since 1915 and have served in various roles. The church and community in Wacahoota are important parts of my spiritual life.

I read the Bible regularly and keep a copy among the few small books I carry with me. It gives me consolation and guidance for my soul, especially in difficult times. My faith is grounded in the teachings of the Bible, which I value above all other reading material.

entity: The Emphasized Gospel of St. John
summary: Among the books I keep is The Emphasized Gospel of St. John, which I read for comfort and spiritual understanding. It is one of the religious texts that help me maintain my faith and find solace. I prefer reading such meaningful works over trivial books.
